Okay, so we're going to be talking about legal stuff. Now, this is a session which I talk about stuff that I have no knowledge of. I have some experience with, but no real expertise in. That's my disclaimer. My disclaimer is this, get a lawyer. But we're going to go through some of the clauses and generally what they mean. There's one clause that I asked my attorney and he didn't even know. But it's a potentially damaging clause, so you need to really check with your own attorney if you see it. It's the indemnification clause we'll get to in a minute. Anyway, okay, a contract is kind of like picking up a chick in a bar. The questions that you're answering in the contract are very similar. What's your name? And so what kind of business are you? What are you, how are you doing business? Are you a corporation or are you an LLC? What's the name of the company? Some guys may insert the location of the company in this part of the contract. Next, I'm single, are you? On this one, you have your representations. I'm not married, I'm not bound to anybody else, I can work for you, I really can, this could work. And the contractor is not aware of any presently existing facts, circumstance or event, yada, yada, yada, right, that would give rise to any breach of any other agreement, right? So basically we're both available, it's legal for him to hire me, it's legal for me to allow myself to be hired by this guy. Then there are representations where we're making statements about who we are, what we do and that kind of stuff, right? So just read through these, these kinds of things are pretty common sense. And it's pretty much boilerplate stuff, but you got to read carefully and if you don't understand any clause, schedule an hour with an attorney and have him walk you through the whole thing. Red line at first, get a yellow highlighter and anything you don't understand or that looks not good to you, just sit down with your attorney and ask him to explain all this stuff. Yeah, microphone? An hour of service online, a friend of mine did this cash out an employment contract, you don't have to have an attorney on file, she just went online and for a monthly fee she gets so many hours of use, so you know what, I was too. Marcella was saying without a microphone that you can go online and get consultation as well, if you want to do that it's certainly going to be cheaper. My attorney, when I first signed up with him, charged me a $2,500 advanced retainer and then deducts whatever time I use from that and then at some point he'll ask me for another $2,500 or whatever. But it's wonderful, if you want to go the way, you know, as cheap as possible with an attorney that's fine, as you get going you're going to want a relationship, you're going to want a guy who understands your business, knows your business. What we do is very different from, you know, what most consultants do and the way most businesses operate. So having an attorney who understands your business is a great idea and somebody you have a relationship with. I'll tell you how weird our business is. Tom Phillips was building a great company but he couldn't get any bank to understand his business model. He had to buy his own bank to finance his receivables and to give himself loans. So that's how weird this is. So having a relationship with an attorney makes a lot of sense. Not to belabor the point but I'm going to belabor the point. I told you the story about security where a coin in bullion, I found out the guy was cheating his customers. I wrote one of the best sales letters I ever wrote that sold the Federal Trade Commission on not coming after me for any of this when I resigned. And they didn't and they didn't even call me in for depositions. The other part of it though that was partly because of my resignation letter, I'm sure. But the other part of it was I had a contract that said that I wasn't responsible for anything that I wrote. I mean, that I can write all I want to say anything I want to as long as I don't show it to anybody or use it to make money, I haven't committed any kind of crime. And my contract made it very clear that it was up to the client to determine that everything they sent to their customers was in legal and regulatory compliance. So it very clearly stated that if there was ever a problem, it was their problem and not mine. So very important clause, we're going to cover it in a minute but there's not much more to cover. That's basically it. Tell them that other story. They ended you up in New York with being questioned. Yeah. Because that's part of this. Yeah, it really is. Okay, probably the worst month of my life. I got a phone call from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. The Commodity Futures, the CFTC is the pit bull of government regulatory agents. They make FDA, FTC, SEC look like pussycats. I mean, these guys are tough. I get a call from them and they said, we would like you to voluntarily come and be deposed. It was a very nice letter. It said basically I could pick the time and location and I could do it in Chicago or I could do it in Manhattan. It was about one of my clients, somebody I had done some work for named Nick Garino. So I called my attorney, Charlie, and he was in Manhattan. So I said, we'll do it in Manhattan. So one year to the day before the 9-11 tragedy, I'm on the 86th floor of the World Trade Center being deposed by the CFTC. And it turns out that what was happening, and this could happen to any one of you guys, I was selling a subscription to his newsletter. He was collecting the money, not delivering the product. I say, I would have no way of knowing this, right? But the product itself, I mean, after the first issue or so, but he was also doing something else. He was going through direct mail and emails to those people who had signed up and he was selling them a completely non-compliant commodities trading program. And so now this guy, to avoid prosecution, moved to... Was it Nicaragua? And they couldn't get to him, and he never came to the states, and they didn't know exactly where he was. And so they needed to understand more about his business, and that's why I was called in. But when you get a letter from the CFTC asking you to voluntarily do something, that is not voluntary. You do it, or they're going to come after you next. So once again, I had a contract with him absolving me of any liability based on any infraction of a regulation or a law, and clearly stated that he and he alone got to make the decision whether or not to present my copy to the marketplace based on his own understanding of the law and regulations. So I... Basically, it cost me $20,000 in a month of worry to deal with that, but you just don't want to be there. This kind of stuff is important, and it gets back to why it's important to have an attorney who understands your business. Okay, so next is what are you looking for? Who are you? Are we both okay to do this thing? No contract prohibits me from doing this, no contract prohibits you from doing this. What are you looking for? What am I supposed to do for you? So itemize the components, and allow me to write you 70 emails, I'm going to write you a squeeze page, I'm going to write you a sales page. What are you going to write? What is the job? I like to see a line in there that's not necessary, absolutely necessary, but I like to see some kind of line that says that this definition may change as the work goes on, because it always does. And there should also be a rough schedule. When am I supposed to deliver this stuff? So everyone's on the same page. The best thing about contracts really isn't that it binds anybody. The best thing about contracts is that it just gets everyone on the same page. We all know what we're talking about, we all know what the job is, we're all agreeing to this, so there can't be a situation later on where you go, oh, I didn't know the deadline was that day, well it's in your freaking contract. So this is really a major purpose of this. Like I said, I've never had to really act on any of these. What's in it for me if I do these things for you? You see the progression here? Who are you? Are we free? What do I have to do? What's in it for me? What are you going to pay me? And again, each of these needs, it has to have the fear advance and the incentive, and also the next slide is when do I get paid? So now everyone's clear, you're not going to have that little surprise that I had when I found out I only got paid semi-annually. Very important. How long do I get paid? So this clause will have stuff sometimes, one of my contracts right now has stuff that says that the client is free to change my copy in any way he wants to and use it in any way he wants to, so long as Clayton gets paid. It's very important. There have been some fairly reputable companies in my industry who have hired copywriters, Kim Krause Schwam and Blinda Carlisle come to mind, who have hired copywriters who wrote copy for them. Then the client paraphrased the copy, basically went through word by word and paraphrased it and went out and said, well, this is our new control. So now they didn't have to pay the royalty anymore. Isn't that a nightmare? You're all excited because you got the control and then next thing you know, your own client has ripped you off. So that clause is really good to have in here too, that talk to your attorney about that. Given that that is a problem with some companies that needs to be very clear that this copy, any time it's used, you get your royalty. I think there's a slide about this too, but just in case. The question comes up, who owns the copy? The answer is the client. He owns the copy, you don't. But that's okay because you have a guarantee in your contract that every single time he uses it, you're going to get a royalty. How do I know you're not cheating? And this is, copywriter's asked this all the time, it's not a big deal, it's in the contract. How do I know? Well, I know because to confirm the accuracy of the mailing, well, first of all, he's got to give me a mailing report at the end of each month that tells me how many names he mailed of my piece or if it's online, what use he's made of my piece and what the results were. And then to confirm the accuracy of this report, you have the right to demand an audit or post office receipts or other documentation that prove his numbers. Now again, especially for the new writers, young writers in the room, it's not in your client's best interest. If you've got a controller, it's not in his best interest to screw you. Just isn't. Two things will happen. Number one, he loses you. Number two, he loses every copywriter that listens to you when you tell them that this guy screwed you. So it's not in their best interest to cheap out on your royalties. So it very rarely happens. I've had it happen twice in 42 years. So very rarely. Here's the pre-nup. Let's say you're working on this thing. This happened to me. I was working on a project. I sent in the first draft copy and the crit came back and it was clear to me I was working with a drilling moron. I just said, this isn't going to work, guys. These crits are idiotic and they'll kill the package. I quit. But I had a kill fee in my contract. It just basically said, I got to keep the advance. I love this because basically the minute I start working on this project, it's like the client offering me a free $12,500 thing if I just pick up the phone and fire him. So that makes clients very polite, very friendly, very interested in helping you succeed, among other things of course. But that's a typical kill fee. Your upfront fee will be paid in two parts. The first part is half and the second part is half. First part paid when you begin the job or when they schedule your time. That's something to think about. A lot of people will sleepwalk through this and they'll say, well, I take half of my upfront fee when I start work. Why? You're reserving the time now. You're blacking that time out so that nobody else could get that time from you. You have no other source of income for that period of time. I want my first half of my advance now, the minute I say yes, because your name is now on my schedule and I can't sell that time to anyone else. First money, please. Second money comes when the client accepts your final draft. At that point the client may say, you know what? We don't like this. I don't see any way to save it and so we're just not going to pay you and we're not going to use it. You get to keep that first half. It's still a kill fee. That has happened to me never. If they've gone, has it Wendy? I don't think a single time. Can't think of a single time. We've had a couple of bizarre things happen where you did get to keep the kill fee. It's never happened that I wrote the copy and it wasn't used that I can remember. If it did happen, it's so rare neither one of us can remember a specific instance. Very, very rare. But still you're protected by your kill fee even if that happens. Yeah. Microphone for Carly? Yes. I'll now. It's important that you do stipulate that you get the balance of your payment when final draft is accepted by the client, not final design or anything else because once you've turned that final copy in, you've done your job. Now it's true you're going to work with the designer to get it finalized but they can hold on to that copy for months before they get it designed so you don't want them holding on to anything. It's also good to say within two weeks. These CFOs are bastards. They will hold your check till hell won't have it just to age the funds. There are some companies that will hold invoices for 60 days or even 90 days. So there's another clause you can put in there that charges them interest if they take more than 30 days to pay you. I've done that too. So you'll pay me within 30 days of the completion of the copy or you'll be liable for a 10% interest on the money until you do pay me. And of course CFOs, they get that. They're all about aging funds. They get that, they look at it and go, oh, we don't want to do that. To us it's pennies, but it's a big deal to them. Okay, next, who owns this stuff? Right? It's a community property paragraph kind of. Who owns this stuff? Okay, well we talked about that. The client owns it. But it just means that all you say is you get paid no matter what. No restriction on companies' use of work. This is that clause I was telling you about. So long as contractor is compensated, that's the key phrase here. As said forth here, the company shall not be restricted in any way from modifying reverse engineering or decompiling the work or creating. You know, look at that reverse engineering. What does that mean? What that means is if they do what Marcella did with my copy, they take my copy, they deconstruct it, and then they rewrite it along the exact same outline. That's not legal for them to do. If they do that, they have to pay me a royalty. What do we call this relationship? We just live in together, we get married, what's the deal? So it's really important to the client that you be clearly identified as an independent contractor. It has all kinds of implications for withholding taxes and all kinds of things. They have to do matching FICA and stuff like that. So they don't want to be caught in that kind of situation. So now with Obamacare and everything, there's all kinds of health insurance questions. If somebody at the IRS or someone else determines you're an employee, then it just opens a whole rat's nest for the company. So this is one of their favorite clauses. It's just fine, it protects you too, and I wouldn't worry too much about it. If your client offers to buy you a computer or to provide your desk or other key pieces of equipment, the IRS may look at that and say, no, you're an employee, and you need to be paying withholding tax, you know, withholding tax, you need to be covered by the insurance plan, you need all of this other stuff. So they want to be protected against that. So always take cash, don't take a new computer or any other hard equipment, you can buy your own stuff. So a lot of these clauses are just baloney, you know. Like for example, contractor determines means and methods. Now see, I know that's baloney because I'm going to take direction from the company, right? And the company knows that's baloney, but any judge looking at this will tell the IRS, well look, you know, he's obviously not an employee, he's determining how he does stuff. So you know, it's just what they do. Company has no control. You see how this goes on and on? You know, it's like the biggest part of the contract, you know. So confidentiality, this is also no big deal. It basically says, look, especially before the copy is used, you don't tell anybody about it. No details, please. After it's used, nobody cares because everyone, you know, all the competitors have seen it and everything. But this is, you know, you just got to make sure that, you know, you're careful during that sensitive period. Now this may extend to all kinds of periods, but the bottom line is the company won't care. If they mailed the piece and a million people have seen it, they're not going to sue you because you told somebody you wrote it, you know. So don't worry about that. Well you can read this stuff, anything. I won't bore you with it. The next one is non-compete. Now if somebody hands you a contract with this in it, you know, just, you know, quit work for the day, go to the bar and have fun because this is a gift. If this client wants you on a non-compete basis, this happened to me with Phillips. You guys know, what is it, the federal farm bank, where they pay you not to grow crops? You know, that's what this is. You know, so Phillips came to me and they said, we don't want you to write for anybody else. And this, of course, this is back in the early 90s, so when $10,000 was $10,000. And so we don't want you to write for anybody else. I said, well okay, I think maybe you can pay me 120,000 a year not to write for anybody else. And it's like getting paid not to grow crops, right? Free money. And so that's what they did. And so if you see a non-compete in a contract, it's time to go back to the negotiating table. I'll be more than happy to do this, but you're going to have to pay for it because, you know, I've spent years developing relationships with other clients. And if you're now going to, if you want to deprive me of the right to work with those people, you're going to have to pay for that. And you know, unless this is just a frivolous thing they've stuck in there, you'll have to go to the negotiating room and you can add some money to your bottom line with this. It was a huge win-win. Thank you very much. Yeah, not only wasn't I doing what I was doing very well for Phillips for their competitors, see every time you do a job there's several things that happen. You're doing the job, but also you're introducing some new ideas or some new technology or some new concepts in the marketing of the product the way you wrote the copy, right? So at that time, for example, one of those things would have been alternative health newsletters. And I could have gone to Agora in a heartbeat and said, okay, let's start an alternative health newsletter. As it was, it took them like two years to figure it out. But so I could have made millions with that information at the time. So asking for a compensation for a non-compete is absolutely good. And I'm going to go on for Marcella. You can also carve a little niche out. One thing I did when I first started was Shavers wanted a non-compete for anything. And I said, well, I'm not going to do that because then I might as well just still be an in-house writer. You know, the whole idea is I want to grow my business. I want to do other things. So I said, look, Paris, I'll do health with him. I won't do option trading services. That doesn't compete with you. He's brilliant. It's going to help what I'm doing for you. So let me do that. And they're like, okay, that sounds fine. And then the next relationship I had was with the Motley Fool. And that was back in the day. Remember when they had the we will die before we'll ever trade an option? So I went to Bernie and I said, look, you share list swaps with the Motley Fool. You guys market to each other. They have this rule that they'll die before they'll ever trade options. So can I go right for them? And I'll just keep doing options for you. So there's ways to slice and dice that it doesn't have to be an all or nothing. If you see an opportunity here to make $20,000, $30,000 a month for signing a non-compete, it could be a blanket non-compete. If you want to still write for health clients, but this is a financial client, you can do that. My non-compete with Weiss right now is a selected non-compete. So and it's very, I made it very specific, okay? The first draft said Clayton is not allowed to write for any financial company. And I said, no, you gotta be more specific than that. And so they came back with Clayton is not allowed to write for any other financial publisher or publisher of financial newsletters, is what it said. So I could theoretically work for Forbes, because they publish a magazine, not a newsletter. I could technically, I can work for the Money Show, which I'm doing for free right now, but I could work for the Money Show and make money with them legally because they do investment conferences, they don't do newsletters. So it's a non-compete that still gives me freedom to go make money with a lot of other people if I want to. Of course I could do health and all that other stuff. So, but again, look at this as an opportunity. If it comes up, they value you enough that they want you to sign this. It's an opportunity for you to sweeten the pot. Okay, this is that responsibility for compliances to keep you out of prison. I can't believe that that guy actually got 20 years. My boss got 20 years on the RICO Act. I mean, he's out now, but it, cuz it happened in like the 80s, or very early 80s. And so he's out, been out for a while. But I mean, that's serious stuff he was doing. And so when I found out about it, I got the heck out of there. That'd be my advice to you too. But this kind of a paragraph, and don't take these paragraphs and just write your own contract. You need to go through a lawyer, okay? Did everyone hear that? I am not a lawyer, okay? And but, so don't take this stuff here and try to cobble together a contract with it. So anyway, responsibility for compliance. Make it clear that don't shoot me, I'm just a piano player. I just write the copy. I don't determine if or when or to whom this material will be sent. I am not a lawyer. I have no knowledge of regulations, of the regulations about this, or even the law. And so, don't shoot me. The company has to take ultimate responsibility. One time I had the clause written so that the company was warranting to me that they would have everything I wrote passed through a compliance officer or an attorney. It was lame. It was health resources. I had them warrant to me that they would have an FDA attorney review and approve everything I wrote. And it also said that I would make whatever changes were required after discussion with the compliance people. So that will help you a lot. Okay, this is a tricky one that you definitely need to talk to your lawyer about. Generally, as I understand it, and I asked my attorney, I said help me with this one. I don't really get it. And he sent me an email that is completely unintelligible. So basically the way I understand it is, hold harmless means if all hell breaks loose, I'm not going to sue my client over it and he's not going to sue me. We hold each other harmless, right? Indemnification as I, and usually it's written this way. Clayton will indemnify and hold harmless, vice research. And then the next paragraph, vice research will indemnify and hold harmless Clayton Makepeace. Indemnification is a little bit trickier because it obligates you to compensate your client. If it says Clayton Makepeace indemnifies vice research, that means that if vice research gets sued, whether it's by an investor, is pissed off about the product or a government agency, that I have to pay their legal bills. And it's not really that simple, but that's as close as I could get to an accurate description. So make sure that you look very carefully. I won't sign a contract that says that I indemnify anybody. And the lawyers will sometimes say, well, Clayton, it's kind of mitigated over here or we took care of it in another way. I said, no, Clayton doesn't indemnify anything. If you guys get in trouble, that's on you. Yeah, Mike for Chris. So would you sign it if, like you said, it had the indemnify and hold harmless for the copywriter and indemnify and hold harmless for each? No, because still I'm indemnifying him. I'm fine with holding him harmless. I'm not going to sue anybody over anything. Okay, here's the nightmare scenario. Guy comes in, you can sue anybody for anything at any time. So a customer comes in and he sues everybody, everything that moves. He sues the company, he sues the owner individually, he sues me, he sues maybe the direct mail manager, sues everybody that was in the chain that got him to buy this product. Now, that's going to cost us money, period. I mean, they may be frivolous lawsuits, they may be the kind of suits that are going to get thrown out of court the first time the judge sees them. But in the meantime, we're going to spend money on attorneys to get ready to fight it. So indemnification, I'm paying his legal bills. Right, and I'm not going to do that. So I'm fine holding him harmless, but I am not going to say, I'm not going to indemnify. Now, if an attorney sat down with me and explained this to me in plain English, I might have a different opinion. But as I understand it now, and I Googled it, I went online, I read on several legal sites, and to my best understanding, it's a real bad idea. So, and I've never done it. I just always say, no, I'm not going to do the indemnification stuff. All right, so if you don't understand something, ask. Don't be afraid of looking dumb. Lawyers are specialists in this kind of stuff. They don't expect you to know this kind of stuff. So ask the questions. Use any clause that shows up in the contract. That you didn't expect. That's the time to slam the brakes on and say, wait a minute guys, we didn't talk about this. If you're going to want that, then I'm going to want this. So anything above and beyond what you discussed prior to getting the contract is an opportunity for renegotiation and to make more money on the deal. And don't always assume that if something like that happens, it was like the client's a sneaky bastard. Sometimes things just don't come out in conversation. So again, the benefit of the doubt and just figure a way to make money with it. Establish a relationship with a good lawyer. We talked about that. Never say anything you don't understand. Ask how compliance is handled. Better yet, have it a clause on compliance in here. I've had a lot of situations in Lane Lowry at Health Resources with one of them. Where when I first met him, I said, who does your compliance? And he goes, well, nobody really. I've got an attorney that's done some FDA work and every once in a while, he'll take a look at my stuff. And I said, well, if I work with you, you're going to have to agree that everything I write goes through that attorney and gets his blessing before we use it. Now, Lane was actually raided by the FDA. I mean, when they raid you, we're talking to M16s and flak jackets. And they can come in, they can grab all your computers, and it's just amazing what they can do. And he was raided, and he still wasn't at that time, closely scrutinizing every word a copy that was going out. But when I came in, I insisted on it. And so everything Carlene and I did with him went through compliance. So you may find yourself in that situation. Great company, great product, guy doesn't know beans about compliance. So you're going to have to say, look, we need to find somebody who used to work for the FDA. An attorney who used to work for the FDA would be best. Failing that, somebody who's defended cases against the FDA successfully. And of course, Lane was in Birmingham. Good luck finding a former FDA attorney there. But we found a good guy. And now on this, let's say you write some copy and the compliance people or the attorney come back to you and they go, you can't say this, you can't say this. Don't just blindly delete the copy. Say, well, what can I say? A good attorney will not tell you what you can't do. He'll tell you how to do what you want or need to do in order to be successful. So they should be identifying problems and then helping you solve them. So don't be intimidated by the attorney. He comes in and says, you can't use this word. Okay, what word can I use? Get their help with it. I have compliance people that try to rewrite copy and they can't even spell. So you don't accept just everything. I mean, I've got this woman that is doing compliance at life, wonderful lady. But she can't spell. And so she'll try to write a disclaimer and she goes, of course, all investment carries risk. And she spells course, C-O-A-R-S-E. So don't just accept the changes at face value. You can negotiate and sometimes they'll read something wrong. They'll read it the way they, some unintended way. And you can go back to them and say, well, that isn't what this passage actually says. Here's what it says. And so now, is that okay or do we need to make other changes? So be an active partner in this. Ultimately, it's their call. You're not in control there, but feel free to fight for your copy. Another way to go is to check with other copywriters who are signing contracts. Just talk to them. If there's a clause you don't understand. Maybe it's just a clause you're not sure is really normal in that industry. Call up somebody else that's writing in that industry and say, hey, do you have this in your contracts? And it'll let you know whether or not that's something you should seriously consider. And I like life signatures personally. There are new laws regarding virtual signatures that make them usable. Microphone back there for Barbara. If you were in negotiations with a company and they said they did not have a compliance department in place, would you walk? I've never had to do that because these companies don't want to go to jail either. So what I have done is I've told them, look, I can't work for you unless there's somebody doing compliance. And it doesn't have to be somebody internally. It can be just an attorney in town who will look at this stuff, right? We at Blanchard and Company, we went through a period of time. And this is aside from contracts, but it's interesting because it's on a legal basis. Be creative in the way that you address these legal and regulatory issues. Back in the 80s, the Federal Trade Commission went on a rampage against rare coin companies. They were dragging people into court right and left. They were putting some people in jail. It was, they just had a vendetta. They saw rare coins as an unregulated investment, which they really are. If I'm selling stocks, I have this many regulations from the SEC I have to follow. I can sell gold coins, I can pretty much say anything I want to, so long as it's not inaccurate or misleading. And so they were just sick of that. So they decided they were gonna make some examples and they started coming after rare coin companies right and left. We did two things in response. So we wanted a creative solution to this. We were the leader. We were the big guy. If anybody was gonna be made an example of it, it was gonna be us. Now we weren't doing any of the bad stuff, but like I said yesterday, you don't have to be. All it takes is the allegation from a government regulator that you're somehow out of compliance or you're somehow fraudulent. And that's gonna go carry with you for a long time. Especially now with the internet, and you look up a company. The company may be the first listing on the results page and there could be ten pages of, is such and such a fraud. And it's on there forever. So we wanted to avoid any problems. So we did two things. First thing was we required that every single word of copy be substantiated. There was any claim of fact at all. And it didn't even have to do with how much a coin had appreciated in value. It could be that we talk about the coin was struck at the Carson City Mint between these years. We had to have third party substantiation for that. So we had a substantiation file for every single project that we did. And the way the government operates is you'll be at work one day and you'll get a phone call from the Federal Trade Commission. And they'll say, we're on the way to the courthouse. We're gonna get a temporary restraining order against you. And what that means is you cannot conduct business. You have to basically shut down. You cannot promote, you cannot sell over the phone. You cannot conduct business if you have a TRO against you, okay? So you're out of business. Now this is with no evidence. This is with no evidence, no hearing, nothing. They just say, we don't like the way you're doing things. We're gonna go get a TRO. We wanted to be able to grab a file, meet them to the courthouse, slap the file on the judge's desk and say every word in that promotion is true. And we got to the place where we could do that. So that was really important. The second thing that we did, and this was brilliant, I'm sorry, but it was really brilliant. You know how you watch these, they have these little spots on TV where there's something from the government, is it working now? From the government printing office, offering you a free report on something? And could be some kind of consumer report on something? And the Colorado Springs thing, you call 800 number and you can get this free report from the government. So I started thinking, okay, why don't I write the definitive consumer guide to investing in rare coins? And have the government offer it, right? So then in order to do this, you have to have a regulator or an agency, the federal government, cosponsor the report with you. So we were doing all of our promotions through the mail. So I picked the post office. So now I've got, there's no way anyone's gonna come against me for mail fraud. I mean, I'm the guy who's so squeaky clean the post office cosponsored my report, warning people about how coin companies rip them off. I mean, it was really great. It took like 60 days from the time we decided to do it to the time it was up there. And I think it's still there. I think if you go to the GPO site, you'd probably find it a consumer's guide to rare coin investment. We were squeaky clean, but again, this really helped position us head and shoulders above the competition. So microphone for Chris. You quick questions, Clayton. When you hold harmless a client, you ask them to hold you harmless. Yes. And when we went through the contract, you could see that it was project based. Yes. So every time new project, new contract is there ever a time where you kind of get into a big umbrella contract or that just doesn't happen? No, no, I mean, I'm under an umbrella contract that renews every three years. Or five years. I think my new one is five years. So in that contract, everything's covered in very different contract. But a lot of these clauses are also in there, the non-competes and hold harmless and stuff like that. But yeah, no, every time I hire you, Chris, you already signed a contract. Next time I hire you, you'll sign another, that same contract again. So each time. Yes, microphone up here. Two quick questions. When you're starting out and you're working with a big company and they give you a contract that they use with other copywriters. And now that I've seen this and possibly something is missing or whatever, would I go back to them and say I want to have my own contract? Or what would I do if they already have a standard one? I would identify any problematic areas. I tell you, did you read the contract that we sent you? Did you actually read it before you signed it? I did, yeah. And you still signed it? There was stuff in there that I wouldn't sign in a million years. And it was our own personnel department and our own legal beagles, our own ambulance chasers and they all got together and came up with this monstrosity that basically requires you to give us a list of every client you've ever had. I mean, there are all kinds of owners clauses in there. And when I saw it for the first time last year, I freaked out. And I said, I would never sign this a million years. And if any copywriter ever asked me if they should sign it, I would tell them no. And not from you, I mean, talking about the company, the other company. Yeah, yeah, well, I was talking to Chris too. Because she signed this thing. So get a lawyer. No, but the answer is, my answer is, don't be nitpicky at that point. If it's a quality company and you know they pay and they don't have a history of screwing copywriters, then only if there's a major point in there that you really don't like. If there was a non-computer that isn't compensated or if there was a indemnification clause that puts you at great risk. But that's why you need to have an attorney look at it, just ask him. And pay for an hour of his time, it can be one of the cheapest things you ever do. Yes, microphone in the back. Do you have another? Very short. Do you also have liability, personal liability insurance for your copywriting? No. No, no, the contract covers it. Yeah, I don't feel I've ever needed it. We have a LLC, a limited liability corporation that does a fairly good job of, there's a kind of a corporate shield there to protect us as individuals if something would have, or to happen. They are always, you can always penetrate those. The government especially can, but we've never felt a need for the insurance. Don't be nitpicky on the contract. If there's some clauses in there that you have questions about but you can live with, live with them. You don't want to be problematic. But if there is a clause in there, you cannot live with. Then I would raise that issue with them. But again, I would go to an attorney first, determine exactly what your liability is from that clause. Or maybe it's not a liability. Maybe you look at the clause and as I said, it's non-compete. So it's something you can say, look, my fees were based on a one project thing only if you want to non-compete. That's a different deal. We're gonna have to work out compensation for it. But you can make a really good point. You don't want to be the problem child here. So only if it's important, only if your attorney feels it's important.
